Description: Uno implements the WinUI API across platforms
Do: Microsoft.UI.Xaml namespace for all UI code
Don't: WPF or Xamarin.Forms namespaces
Code Bad: using System.Windows.Controls; or using Xamarin.Forms;

Description: Not all WinUI APIs are implemented on every platform
Do: Uno API compatibility docs before using new APIs
Don't: Assuming all WinUI APIs work everywhere
Code Bad: Using unimplemented API and discovering at runtime

Guideline: Use Uno.WinUI not Uno.UI for new projects
Description: Uno.WinUI uses WinUI 3 APIs
Do: Uno.WinUI NuGet packages for new projects
Don't: Uno.UI (UWP API surface) for new projects
Code Bad: <PackageReference Include="Uno.UI"/> (legacy UWP API surface)

Description: Speed up development with live XAML editing
Do: Hot Reload for iterating on layouts
Don't: Restarting app for every XAML change
Code Good: Click Hot Reload button in VS toolbar or save in VS Code/Rider to apply XAML changes
Code Bad: Full rebuild for margin tweak

Description: Conditional namespaces for platform-specific UI
Do: xmlns:android xmlns:ios xmlns:wasm for platform XAML
Don't: Shared XAML when platforms need different controls
Code Bad: #if in code-behind to set text per platform

Guideline: Use partial classes for platform code
Description: Separate platform implementations in partial files
Do: Partial class files with platform-specific logic
Don't: #if directives in shared code for large blocks
Code Bad: #if __IOS__ ... #elif __ANDROID__ ... 100-line blocks in shared file

Description: Target correct platforms with defines
Do: __IOS__ __ANDROID__ __WASM__ __DESKTOP__ for platform checks
Don't: Inventing custom symbols or checking OS at runtime
Code Bad: if (RuntimeInformation.IsOSPlatform(OSPlatform.Android)) for compile-time choice

Description: Keep shared code maximized
Do: Abstract platform differences behind interfaces
Don't: Duplicating logic across platform files
Code Bad: Same 50 lines copy-pasted into iOS and Android partial classes

Description: Standard WinUI navigation pattern
Do: Frame.Navigate with page types
Don't: Manual content swapping
Code Bad: contentPresenter.Content = new DetailPage();

Description: Type-safe navigation with DI integration
Do: Uno.Extensions navigation for complex apps
Don't: Manual Frame management in large apps
Code Bad: Frame.Navigate with string parsing everywhere

Description: SystemNavigationManager.BackRequested works on Android iOS and WASM but is unimplemented on WinAppSDK desktop where calling GetForCurrentView() throws at runtime
Do: Subscribe to BackRequested only on platforms that support it or use Uno.Toolkit NavigationBar for cross-platform back UX
Don't: Calling SystemNavigationManager.GetForCurrentView() on WinUI 3 desktop without a guard
Code Bad: SystemNavigationManager.GetForCurrentView().BackRequested += OnBackRequested; with no platform guard — crashes on Windows desktop

Description: Support URI activation across platforms
Do: Handle protocol activation and URI routing
Don't: Single entry point ignoring activation
Code Good: Route URIs to specific pages on activation
Code Bad: Ignoring OnLaunched activation args

Description: Uno offers both rendering approaches
Do: Skia for pixel-perfect cross-platform consistency
Don't: Assuming native rendering on all platforms
Code Bad: Expecting platform-native controls on Skia targets

Description: Uno 5.2+ ships a single Skia Desktop shell that auto-selects X11 Win32 or AppKit per OS — Skia.Gtk Skia.Linux.Framebuffer and Skia.WPF heads are deprecated
Do: net10.0-desktop TFM with UnoPlatformHostBuilder for cross-platform desktop
Don't: Targeting the legacy Skia.Gtk or Skia.Linux.Framebuffer heads in new projects
Code Bad: Per-OS Skia.Gtk Skia.MacOS Skia.Linux.Framebuffer head projects

Description: Visual differences exist between renderers
Do: Visual testing on each active target platform
Don't: Testing only on Windows assuming others match
Code Good: Screenshot tests on iOS Android WASM and Desktop
Code Bad: Testing only on Windows Desktop

Description: Access native APIs through Uno abstractions
Do: Native platform APIs via platform-specific code
Don't: Avoiding native features for purity
Code Good: #if __IOS__ UIKit API call #endif for camera access
Code Bad: Pure shared code that avoids using the camera

Description: WebAssembly downloads can be large
Do: IL linker and AOT for smaller WASM bundles
Don't: Default settings for production WASM
Code Bad: Publishing WASM without linker

Description: Defer element creation until needed
Do: x:Load=False for hidden panels and tabs
Don't: Loading all UI elements upfront
Code Bad: Always-loaded Collapsed panels

Description: Compiled bindings eliminate runtime reflection — Uno supports x:Bind across iOS Android WASM Skia and Windows targets that compile XAML so prefer it over {Binding} for static well-typed bindings
Do: x:Bind for property and event bindings; reserve {Binding} for runtime-typed DataContext scenarios
Don't: {Binding} everywhere when x:Bind would compile
Code Bad: <TextBlock Text="{Binding Title}"/> for a statically known property

Description: Performance characteristics vary by target
Do: Platform-specific profiling tools
Don't: Assuming desktop perf equals mobile
Code Good: Instruments on iOS and Android Profiler on Android
Code Bad: Profiling only on Windows

Description: Consistent theming across platforms
Do: ThemeResource for adaptive colors
Don't: Hardcoded colors per platform
Code Bad: Background="#FFFFFF"

Description: Application.RequestedTheme accepts only ApplicationTheme.Light/Dark — to follow the system theme leave it unset entirely. ElementTheme.Default exists only on FrameworkElement.RequestedTheme not on Application
Do: Omit Application.RequestedTheme so the OS theme wins; use RequestedTheme=Default on FrameworkElement to inherit from parent
Don't: Setting Application.RequestedTheme=""Default"" — not a valid ApplicationTheme value and throws at parse time
Code Good: <Application></Application> with RequestedTheme unset; <Grid RequestedTheme="Default">...</Grid> on a FrameworkElement
Code Bad: <Application RequestedTheme="Default"> // not a valid ApplicationTheme

Description: Override control sub-properties via resources
Do: Lightweight styling keys for minor tweaks
Don't: Full ControlTemplate for small changes
Code Bad: Copying entire ControlTemplate to change one color

Description: Theme rendering differs across platforms
Do: Visual theme testing on all targets
Don't: Assuming themes look identical everywhere
Code Good: Screenshot comparison across platforms for themed controls
Code Bad: Theming only tested on Windows

Description: Separate view and logic
Do: CommunityToolkit.Mvvm or Prism for MVVM
Don't: Code-behind for business logic
Code Good: [ObservableProperty] public partial string Title { get; set; } [RelayCommand] private void Save() { }
Code Bad: MainPage.xaml.cs with all logic

Description: Official extension libraries for common patterns
Do: Uno.Extensions for DI navigation configuration
Don't: Building infrastructure from scratch
Code Bad: Manual DI and navigation setup

Description: Register services for testability
Do: Microsoft.Extensions.DI through Uno.Extensions
Don't: Static service locators and singletons
Code Bad: ApiService.Instance or new ApiService() in ViewModels

Description: Maximize code reuse across targets
Do: Business logic in .NET Standard or shared library
Don't: Business logic in platform head projects
Code Good: MyApp.Core class library referenced by all heads
Code Bad: Business logic in MyApp.Wasm.csproj

Description: Single source SVG to multi-platform assets
Do: UnoImage for automatic asset generation from SVG
Don't: Manual asset export per resolution and platform
Code Bad: Manually exporting icon_1x.png icon_2x.png icon_3x.png per platform

Description: Enable screen readers across platforms
Do: AutomationProperties.Name on interactive controls
Don't: Controls without accessible names
Code Bad: <Button><SymbolIcon Symbol="Accept"/></Button> without name

Description: Each platform has different assistive tech
Do: Test with VoiceOver TalkBack and Narrator
Don't: Testing accessibility on one platform only
Code Good: VoiceOver on iOS + TalkBack on Android + Narrator on Windows
Code Bad: Only testing with Narrator on Windows

Description: Respect user font size preferences
Do: Dynamic font scaling for all text
Don't: Fixed font sizes ignoring accessibility
Code Bad: FontSize="14" everywhere

Description: Test business logic independently
Do: xUnit or MSTest on shared ViewModel code
Don't: UI testing only
Code Bad: Manual testing on each platform

Description: Cross-platform UI testing framework
Do: Uno.UITest for automated UI tests across platforms
Don't: Manual regression testing
Code Bad: Manual click-through on each platform

Guideline: Show an extended splash screen on WASM
Description: WASM bundle download and runtime startup take several seconds on first load — render branded UI immediately so users do not see a blank page (AOT and trimming are covered separately)
Do: Render a splash overlay in wwwroot/index.html that hides on first XAML navigation
Don't: Letting the user wait on a blank white page while the runtime boots
Code Good:
index.html:
Loading…
hidden via JS interop after first Frame.Navigate
Code Bad: No splash markup in index.html — 5-second blank page on first visit

Description: Ahead-of-time compilation improves runtime speed
Do: AOT for production WASM builds
Don't: Interpreter mode in production
Code Bad: Default interpreter mode in production deployment

Description: WASM runs in browser sandbox
Do: Feature detection for browser APIs
Don't: Assuming desktop capabilities in browser
Code Bad: #if __WASM__ StorageFile.GetFileFromPathAsync("C:/data") #endif

Description: WinUI NavigationView for consistent navigation across platforms
Do: NavigationView with MenuItems for app navigation
Don't: Custom hamburger menu implementation
Code Bad: Custom SplitView with manual toggle button

Description: Cross-platform modal dialogs using WinUI API
Do: ContentDialog for confirmations and input
Don't: Custom overlay Panel as dialog
Code Bad: Grid overlay with manual focus trapping

Description: Standard command bar with primary and secondary commands
Do: CommandBar with AppBarButtons for toolbar actions
Don't: Custom StackPanel toolbar
Code Bad: <StackPanel Orientation="Horizontal"><Button>Save</Button></StackPanel>

Description: Platform-native toggle control for on/off preferences
Do: ToggleSwitch for settings and feature flags
Don't: CheckBox for toggle settings
Code Bad: <CheckBox Content="Enable dark mode"/>

Description: Enable UI updates when ViewModel properties change
Do: CommunityToolkit.Mvvm [ObservableProperty] for auto-notification
Don't: Properties without change notification
Code Good: [ObservableProperty] public partial string Title { get; set; }
Code Bad: public string Title { get; set; } without notification

Description: Collection change notifications for ItemsSources across platforms
Do: ObservableCollection<T> for data-bound lists
Don't: List<T> for bound ItemsSources
Code Bad: List<Item> Items { get; set; } = new();

Description: iOS and Android may suspend or terminate the app — WinAppSDK desktop does not raise Suspending so use window Closed for desktop save-state
Do: Save state in OnSuspending and restore on activation
Don't: Ignoring lifecycle losing user state on mobile
Code Good: Application.Current.Suspending += (s, e) => { var d = e.SuspendingOperation.GetDeferral(); SaveState(); d.Complete(); };
Code Bad: No suspend handler losing form data on mobile

Description: Structured app initialization with DI and configuration
Do: IHost builder pattern for app startup and service registration
Don't: Manual initialization in App constructor
Code Bad: new MainViewModel() in App.xaml.cs constructor

Guideline: Use ListView virtualization for large lists
Description: Only renders visible items to reduce memory and layout cost
Do: ListView with default ItemsStackPanel virtualization
Don't: ItemsControl or StackPanel for large data sets
Code Bad: <ItemsControl><StackPanel> rendering 5000 items at once

Description: Skia and WinAppSDK targets need full keyboard operability — note TabIndex routing is not fully implemented on every Uno target
Do: AccessKey and KeyboardAccelerator on Skia and WinAppSDK targets
Don't: Mouse-only interactions on desktop
Code Bad: Clickable controls without keyboard support on desktop

Guideline: Use service workers for offline support
Description: Enable PWA capabilities for WASM deployments
Do: Service worker registration for caching and offline mode
Don't: Online-only WASM app with no offline fallback
Code Bad: No service worker leaving WASM app unusable offline

Guideline: Marshal to UI thread with DispatcherQueue
Description: Cross-thread access to UI elements throws — capture the UI DispatcherQueue once and use TryEnqueue to update from background work
Do: DispatcherQueue.GetForCurrentThread().TryEnqueue from background work
Don't: Touching UI controls directly from a Task
Code Bad: await Task.Run(() => StatusText.Text = "Done"); throws on non-UI thread

Description: Required for Fluent control styles to load — without it controls render with no template
Do: Add XamlControlsResources at the top of Application.Resources MergedDictionaries
Don't: Skipping the merged dictionary and wondering why Buttons look unstyled
Code Bad: <Application.Resources><SolidColorBrush x:Key="MyBrush" Color="Red"/></Application.Resources> with no XamlControlsResources merged

Description: AsyncRelayCommand reports CanExecute=false (raising CanExecuteChanged) and exposes IsRunning while the Task is in flight — the bound control is disabled and re-entrancy is prevented by default (AllowConcurrentExecutions=false)
Do: [RelayCommand] on a Task-returning method for awaitable work
Don't: async void event handlers calling .Wait() or .Result
Code Good: [RelayCommand] private async Task LoadAsync() { Items = await _api.GetAsync(); }
Code Bad: public void OnLoadClick(object s, EventArgs e) { LoadAsync().Wait(); } deadlock risk

Description: WinUI x:Uid resolves UI text from .resw resources at runtime — use it instead of hardcoded strings to support localization across iOS Android WASM and desktop from a single project
Do: x:Uid on every user-facing string with matching .resw entries per language under Strings/{lang}/Resources.resw
Don't: Hardcoding language-specific strings into XAML or code-behind
Code Bad: <Button Content="Submit"/> hardcoded in XAML

Description: Cross-platform logging routes to platform-native sinks (OSLog on iOS Console on WASM Debug elsewhere) when configured through the IHost builder
Do: Inject ILogger<T> into ViewModels and services and call UseLogging() on the host builder
Don't: Console.WriteLine or platform-specific log APIs scattered across shared code
Code Good: Host.CreateDefaultBuilder().UseLogging(c => c.SetMinimumLevel(LogLevel.Information)) and ILogger<MainViewModel> via constructor injection
Code Bad: Console.WriteLine("error") in shared code with no platform-aware routing

Description: Skia Desktop on .NET 10 supports Native AOT for faster cold start and smaller deployments — opt in per-target so debug builds remain fast
Do: <PublishAot>true</PublishAot> in a TFM-conditional PropertyGroup for net10.0-desktop release builds
Don't: Enabling PublishAot globally and breaking debug iteration on every TFM
Code Bad: <PublishAot>true</PublishAot> at root with no TFM/Configuration condition

Guideline: Never block on async with .Result or .Wait()
Description: Blocking on a Task from the UI thread deadlocks because the awaiter cannot resume on the captured SynchronizationContext — always await async APIs through to the event handler
Do: Await async methods all the way up; in libraries call ConfigureAwait(false) to avoid context capture
Don't: Calling .Result .Wait() or GetAwaiter().GetResult() on a Task from the UI thread
Code Good: private async void OnLoadClick(object s, RoutedEventArgs e) { var data = await _api.GetAsync(); Items = data; }
Code Bad: private void OnLoadClick(object s, RoutedEventArgs e) { var data = _api.GetAsync().Result; } // deadlocks on UI thread

Guideline: Define ThemeDictionaries for Light Dark and HighContrast
Description: Resources placed inside ResourceDictionary.ThemeDictionaries entries are automatically swapped when the system theme changes — required for theme-aware brushes
Do: Wrap brushes in a ThemeDictionaries dictionary keyed by Light Dark and HighContrast in App.xaml or page resources
Don't: Defining a single brush at the root and missing dark/high-contrast variants
Code Bad: <SolidColorBrush x:Key="Brand" Color="#005A9E"/> at root with no theme variants

Description: Uno.Sdk is the modern single-project SDK that auto-resolves Uno.WinUI Uno.Toolkit Material and other packages from a UnoFeatures property — declare features by name instead of hand-managing dozens of PackageReferences
Do: Declare features in the csproj via <UnoFeatures>...</UnoFeatures> and let the SDK resolve transitive packages
Don't: Hand-adding every Uno.* PackageReference and matching version numbers across packages
Code Bad: <PackageReference Include="Uno.WinUI"/><PackageReference Include="Uno.Material.WinUI"/> ... duplicated per feature with mismatched versions

Guideline: Persist desktop window state via Window.Closed
Description: WinAppSDK and Skia desktop heads do not raise Application.Suspending — handle the Window.Closed event (and AppWindow size/position changes) to save user state when desktop apps shut down
Do: Subscribe to MainWindow.Closed and persist any unsaved state before the window is destroyed
Don't: Relying on Application.Suspending to fire on desktop targets
Code Bad: Application.Current.Suspending += SaveState; // never fires on WinAppSDK or Skia desktop

Guideline: Use WinRT.Interop for native window handle on Windows
Description: Calling Win32 APIs from a WinUI Window (file pickers icon embedding etc.) requires the HWND — retrieve it via WinRT.Interop.WindowNative.GetWindowHandle and guard the call so non-Windows targets stay unaffected
Do: GetWindowHandle inside a #if WINDOWS block when you need the HWND
Don't: Calling WinRT.Interop in shared code without a platform guard
Code Bad: var hWnd = WinRT.Interop.WindowNative.GetWindowHandle(MainWindow); // breaks build on iOS Android WASM
